expected_findings.summary: Passive K-1 loss from Synthetic Partners 15 LP must be suspended
expected_findings.detail: The K-1 is marked passive and has a loss. Per IRC §469 passive losses can only offset passive income. If there is no passive income to absorb the loss this year, it must be suspended on Form 8582 and carried forward.

expected_findings.summary: PTET election can effectively uncap the SALT deduction on K-1 income
expected_findings.detail: IRS Notice 2020-75 blessed state-level PTET regimes as a way to pay state tax at the entity level, converting a capped SALT deduction into a federal ordinary business expense that passes through as lower K-1 income. Consider the PTET election with each state where the K-1 generates income.

A prior year state refund is only taxable to the extent it produced a federal tax benefit (the §111 tax benefit rule). If prior year was at the SALT cap, the refund is usually NOT taxable. Do the worksheet.

expected_findings.summary: Parker has up to $7,000 of TY2025 IRA headroom
expected_findings.detail: Parker is 38 years old; the TY2025 IRA contribution limit is $7,000. Deductibility may phase out at high AGI for traditional IRA if covered by an employer plan; Roth IRA phases out at $236K–$246K MFJ.

expected_findings.summary: AGI above Roth IRA phaseout — consider backdoor Roth
expected_findings.detail: AGI $419,900 is above the single Roth IRA phaseout ceiling ($165,000). Direct Roth contributions are not allowed. Consider the backdoor Roth strategy (nondeductible traditional IRA contribution + Roth conversion) if there is no pre-tax IRA balance to trigger pro-rata.
Parker has contributed $14,000 toward the TY2025 §402(g) limit of $23,500. Another $9,500 can still be deferred before year end.

expected_findings.summary: Pass-through income allocated across 2+ PTET states
expected_findings.detail: Multiple states allow a PTET election but apportion the deduction differently. Consult the partnership's state apportionment schedule before electing — the federal benefit stacks per state.
Child Tax Credit phases out $50 per $1,000 of AGI above the phaseout start ($200,000 single).

expected_findings.summary: Dependent care credit available (20% at high AGI)
expected_findings.detail: Up to $3,000 of qualified dependent-care expenses can support the credit, though at AGI > $43K the rate is 20%. Requires both spouses to have earned income in MFJ.

expected_findings.summary: Large SALT deduction — run Form 6251 to check AMT exposure
expected_findings.detail: Total state + property tax is $25,200. SALT is added back for AMT under §56(b)(1), which can tip a high-income household into AMT even at TY2025 exemption levels.

expected_findings.summary: Underpayment: need $69,300 withheld for 110% safe harbor
expected_findings.detail: Prior year AGI exceeded $150,000, so the §6654 safe harbor requires 110% of prior year total tax ($69,300) to be paid via withholding or quarterly estimates. Currently $50,400 is withheld; consider a Q4 estimated payment or increased withholding of $18,900 to avoid the §6654 penalty.

expected_findings.summary: Need $68,024 withheld to hit 90% current year safe harbor
expected_findings.detail: The alternative safe harbor is 90% of current year tax. This is usually only easier to hit than 110% prior year if income dropped.

Based on a shortfall of $17,624 and the TY2025 applicable interest rate of ~8%, the underpayment penalty is approximately $705. Consider the annualized income method (Schedule AI) if income was lumpy across quarters.

expected_findings.summary: Use Schedule AI to reduce §6654 penalty on lumpy income
expected_findings.detail: Income arrived unevenly (RSU vest, K-1 distribution, or both). Form 2210 Schedule AI lets you annualize quarterly income so early quarters with little income do not generate a penalty.
